q: The degree of hotness or coldness of an object is known as its:
sol: Temperature is the reliable measure of how hot or cold an object is.

q: Which device is used to measure body temperature?
sol: A clinical thermometer is specifically designed to measure human body temperature.

q: What is the normal temperature of a healthy human body?
sol: The average internal temperature of a healthy human is 37°C (98.6°F).

q: The range of a clinical thermometer is usually:
sol: It covers a narrow range because human body temperature doesn't go below 35°C or above 42°C.

q: Why is there a 'kink' (constriction) in a clinical thermometer?
o: To prevent mercury level from falling on its own
sol: The kink prevents the mercury from flowing back into the bulb immediately, giving time to read the temperature.

q: Which liquid is commonly used in thermometers?
sol: Mercury is used because it expands uniformly and does not stick to the glass.

q: A laboratory thermometer generally has a range from:
sol: Laboratory thermometers need a wider range for scientific experiments.

q: While reading a laboratory thermometer, it should be kept:
sol: It must be kept vertical and read while the bulb is still in contact with the substance.

q: The boiling point of pure water at sea level is:
sol: Standard boiling point of water is 100°C.

q: Freezing point of water is:
sol: Water turns to ice at 0°C on the Celsius scale.

q: Digital thermometers are preferred today because they do not contain:
sol: Mercury is a toxic substance and difficult to dispose of if the thermometer breaks.

q: Before using a clinical thermometer, you should give it a few jerks to:
sol: Jerking forces the mercury back through the kink into the bulb.

q: Heat always flows from:
sol: Heat transfer naturally occurs from a higher temperature region to a lower one.

q: SI unit of temperature is:
sol: Kelvin (K) is the International System unit for temperature.

q: Can we use a clinical thermometer to measure the temperature of boiling milk?
o: Only if it is made of plastic
sol: Boiling milk is much hotter than 42°C; the pressure of expanding mercury will break the clinical thermometer.

q: The expansion of a substance on heating is called:
sol: Substances generally increase in volume when their temperature rises.

q: Maximum-Minimum thermometers are used to measure:
sol: They record the highest and lowest temperatures of the previous day.

q: Stainless steel spoons are used in hot tea. The handle becomes hot due to:
sol: In solids, heat is transferred from the hotter end to the colder end by conduction.

q: Which of the following is an insulator of heat?
sol: Wood does not allow heat to pass through it easily.

q: The level of mercury ________ when the bulb comes in contact with a hot object.
sol: Heat causes mercury to expand and move up the capillary tube.

q: To get an accurate reading, the eye must be ________ with the mercury level.
sol: Viewing from the same level prevents parallax error.

q: In a laboratory thermometer, why do we read it while it is in the liquid?
o: Because it does NOT have a kink
sol: Without a kink, the mercury level falls as soon as it is removed from the hot substance.

q: Temperature of 100°C is equal to ________ in Fahrenheit.
sol: The boiling point of water is 212°F.

q: 0°C on Celsius scale is ________ on Fahrenheit scale.
sol: Freezing point of water is 32°F.

q: The metallic strip used in some thermometers is based on:
sol: Two different metals expand at different rates, causing the strip to bend.

q: Alcohol thermometers are better for very cold regions because:
o: Alcohol has a very low freezing point
sol: Alcohol remains liquid at temperatures where mercury would freeze.

q: One division on a thermometer scale usually represents:
sol: Small divisions allow for precise measurements.

q: We feel 'cold' when:
sol: Sensation of cold is due to the loss of heat from our body to the surroundings.

q: Which of these is NOT a scale of temperature?
sol: Newton is a unit of force, not temperature.

q: A clinical thermometer should be washed with:
sol: Antiseptic kills germs; boiling water might break the thermometer.

q: Gaps are left between railway tracks to allow for:
sol: Rails expand when hot; without gaps, they would bend and cause accidents.

q: When we touch an ice cube, heat flows from:
sol: Your hand is warmer (37°C) than ice (0°C), so heat flows out of your hand.

q: The transfer of heat in liquids and gases is mainly by:
sol: Convection involves the actual movement of particles.

q: Heat from the Sun reaches Earth by:
sol: Radiation does not require a medium to travel through space.

q: Woolen clothes keep us warm because wool is a:
sol: Wool and the air trapped in it prevent body heat from escaping.

q: Dark colored clothes are preferred in:
sol: Dark colors absorb more heat from the sun.

q: A cold steel spoon is dipped in a cup of hot milk. It transfers heat to its other end by the process of:
sol: Conduction is the mode of heat transfer in metals.

q: Which of the following is a good conductor of heat?
sol: Metals like copper are excellent conductors.

q: Sea breeze blows during:
sol: During the day, land heats up faster, causing cool air from the sea to move towards land.

q: Land breeze blows from:
sol: At night, land cools faster than the sea.

q: Blowing on a hot cup of tea helps it cool down by increasing:
sol: Moving air carries away the heat and vapor faster.

q: Which of these feels coldest at 10°C?
sol: Iron is a good conductor and pulls heat away from your hand faster.

q: The temperature of an object is 310 K. In Celsius it is:
A thermometer bulb should not touch the ________ of the beaker while measuring.
sol: Touching the container gives the temperature of the container, not the liquid.

q: Expansion is maximum in ________.
sol: Gas particles are far apart and expand the most when heated.

q: Thermometers work on the principle of:
sol: The change in volume of a liquid with temperature change.

q: Light colored clothes ________ most of the heat that falls on them.
sol: Reflecting heat keeps the body cool in summer.

q: Which material is used to make handles of pressure cookers?
sol: Bakelite is a poor conductor of heat.

q: What happens to the molecules of a substance when it is heated?
sol: Heat increases the kinetic energy of the molecules.

q: The unit of heat is:
sol: Heat is a form of energy, so its SI unit is Joule (J).

q: A wooden spoon is dipped in a cup of ice cream. Its other end:
sol: Wood is an insulator, so heat/cold is not conducted through it.

q: The Celsius scale was designed by:
sol: The scale is named after the Swedish astronomer.

q: Why do we wear more layers of clothing in winter?
o: Layers trap air which is an insulator
sol: Trapped air prevents heat loss from the body.

q: A marble floor feels colder than a carpet in winter because marble:
o: Is a better conductor of heat than carpet
Is a worse conductor of heat than carpet
sol: Marble conducts heat away from your feet faster than a carpet does.

q: Which thermometer has a higher range?
sol: Laboratory thermometers usually go from -10°C to 110°C.

q: Which part of the clinical thermometer is placed under the tongue?
sol: The bulb contains the mercury that senses the heat.

q: In which mode of heat transfer is no medium required?
sol: Radiation can travel through a vacuum.

q: Water is a ________ conductor of heat.
sol: Liquids are generally poor conductors compared to metals.

q: Two objects at the same temperature are in contact. Heat flow will be:
sol: Heat only flows if there is a temperature difference.

q: Why do birds fluff their feathers in winter?
sol: Fluffing creates air pockets that keep them warm.

q: Mercury is a ________.
sol: It is the only metal that is liquid at room temperature.

q: 1 calorie is approximately equal to:
sol: This is the conversion factor between heat units.

q: When a solid changes into a liquid, it is called:
sol: Melting occurs when a substance absorbs heat and changes phase.

q: The temperature at which a liquid changes to gas is its:
sol: For water, this is 100°C.

q: Why should we not hold a thermometer by its bulb while reading it?
o: The reading will change due to our body heat
sol: Your hand's temperature will influence the mercury level.

q: In convection, which part of the fluid moves up?
sol: Hot fluids are less dense and rise up.

q: A thermos flask prevents heat loss by:
sol: Vacuum and reflective surfaces block all heat transfer methods.

q: Which of the following expands the least on heating?
sol: Solids expand much less than liquids or gases.

q: The shine of a thermometer is due to ________.
sol: Mercury is an opaque, shiny liquid metal.

q: What is the relation between Celsius (C) and Fahrenheit (F)?
sol: Standard conversion formula.

q: Body temperature is usually checked for ________ minute(s).
sol: One minute is generally sufficient for a clinical thermometer to stabilize.

q: In winter, the temperature of the surroundings is ________ than body temperature.
sol: Ambient temperature is usually 10-25°C, while body is 37°C.

q: Metals are good conductors because they have:
sol: Free electrons help in fast transfer of energy.

q: Which of these is a sensation of heat?
sol: Skin can detect temperature differences.

q: The sun heats the Earth's surface. This heat moves to the air just above it by:
sol: Air in direct contact with the hot ground is heated by conduction.

q: An empty glass bottle is heated and its mouth is dipped in water. Bubbles come out due to:
sol: Heating causes air inside to expand and escape as bubbles.

q: Temperature of a substance ________ during a state change (like melting).
sol: Heat is used as 'latent heat' to break bonds, so temperature doesn't rise.

q: A tight metal cap on a glass jar can be opened by:
sol: Heat makes the metal cap expand, making it loose.

q: Which is more reliable for checking fever?
sol: Touch is relative and can be misleading; thermometers are accurate.

q: Ventilators in houses are provided near the ceiling because:
sol: Hot, stale air rises and escapes through high openings by convection.

q: Which scale has 180 divisions between freezing and boiling points?
sol: Fahrenheit goes from 32 to 212 (difference of 180).

q: Heat and temperature are the same thing.
sol: Heat is energy; temperature is the measure of the intensity of that energy.

q: The handle of a pressure cooker is made of Bakelite because it is a ________.
sol: It prevents heat from the metal cooker from reaching your hand.

q: Mercury thermometer was invented by:
sol: Fahrenheit invented the first mercury-in-glass thermometer.

q: A clinical thermometer can be used for:
sol: Its range and design are specific to medical use.

q: Air is a ________ conductor of heat.
sol: Gases are very poor conductors.

q: A black surface ________ more heat than a white surface.
sol: Black objects are good absorbers of radiant energy.

q: If we mix 1 litre of water at 30°C with 1 litre of water at 50°C, the temperature will be:
sol: The final temperature will be the average (around 40°C).

q: Thermostat is a device that:
sol: It maintains a constant temperature in appliances like irons or ACs.

q: One scale on a thermometer is marked from 0 to 1. If there are 10 small divisions, each mark is:
sol: 1 divided by 10 = 0.1.

q: Bimetallic strips are used in:
sol: They act as switches that turn off the heat when it gets too high.

q: Which thermometer is used by doctors?
sol: Clinical thermometers are for medical diagnostics.

q: The space between two walls of a thermos flask is a ________.
sol: Vacuum prevents heat transfer by conduction and convection.

q: What is the unit of temperature in the Celsius scale?
sol: It is measured in degrees Celsius.

q: While using a thermometer, it should be ________.
sol: Hygiene is important for medical tools.

q: Water expands when frozen into ice. This is ________.
sol: Usually, substances contract when freezing, but water expands.

q: In which state of matter is convection not possible?
sol: Particles in solids cannot move from one place to another.

q: Which of these is the hottest part of a candle flame?
sol: The outer blue zone has the highest temperature.

q: Pyrometers are used to measure:
sol: They measure heat through radiation from a distance.

q: Why does a metal chair feel colder than a plastic chair in the same room?
o: Metal conducts heat faster from our body
sol: Our perception of cold is often based on how fast we lose heat.
